entries.notes: Absolutive (ang-set) 2nd-person singular enclitic pronoun. Attaches after the predicate. Equivalent to Tagalog 'ka' (post-verbal position) / 'ikaw' (sentence-initial). Do not confuse with the ligature 'ka-' prefix.

examples.en: You (all) go to the market now.

entries.notes: Absolutive (ang-set) 2nd-person plural pronoun. Also used as a polite/respectful singular when addressing elders or people of higher status, parallel to Tagalog 'kayo'. Enclitic; follows the predicate.

examples.en: There are three children. / The children are three.
They are going to the town.

examples.kp: Lukluk la reng tau king silya.
examples.en: The people sat on the chairs.

entries.notes: Absolutive (ang-set) 3rd-person plural enclitic pronoun. Corresponds to Tagalog 'sila'. Follows the predicate or number as an enclitic. This is the key pronoun needed to make sentences like 'Atlu la reng anak' fully parseable.

examples.en: We (not you) are going to church.

entries.notes: Absolutive (ang-set) 1st-person plural exclusive pronoun — 'we' not including the listener. Distinct from 'tamu' (we inclusive, already in dictionary). Some Kapampangan sources also use 'ke' as a reduced variant; 'kami' is the full standard form. Cognate with Tagalog 'kami'.

Singular absolutive (subject/topic) article — the Kapampangan equivalent of Tagalog 'ang' for singular nouns. Marks the topic of the sentence. Contrast with 'ning' (non-subject/genitive) and 'reng'/'ding' (plural subject). One of the most frequent words in Kapampangan.

examples.en: The eyes of the woman are beautiful.

examples.kp: Sali ku ning prutas king palengke.
examples.en: I will buy fruit at the market.
The house of the wealthy person is big.

entries.notes: Singular non-subject (genitive/oblique) article — the Kapampangan equivalent of Tagalog 'ng'. Marks possessors, objects of action, and non-topic nouns. Contrast with 'ing' (subject/topic article). Some sources also describe it as the genitive article.

examples.kp: Lukluk la reng tau king silya.
examples.en: The people sat on the chairs.

examples.kp: Munta la reng kapatad ku king palengke.
examples.en: My siblings are going to the market.

entries.notes: Plural absolutive (subject/topic) article. A phonological sandhi variant of 'ding' — 'ding' is used when the preceding word ends in a vowel, while 'reng' (from 'ring'/'reng') is used after consonants, though the two are often used interchangeably in modern speech. Corresponds to Tagalog 'ang mga'. Critical for pluralizing topic nouns.

examples.kp: Mura ding gulay king palengke ngeni.
examples.en: Vegetables are cheap at the market today.

entries.notes: Plural absolutive (subject/topic) article, the base form alongside 'reng'. Traditionally 'ding' follows vowel-final words and 'reng' follows consonant-final words (sandhi rule), but the two are often used interchangeably in contemporary Kapampangan. Both mean 'the' (plural). See also 'reng'.

examples.en: There are many flowers in the garden.

entries.notes: Plural non-subject (oblique/genitive) article, corresponding to Tagalog 'ng mga'. Used when the plural noun is not the topic of the sentence — e.g., as an object or possessor. Contrast with 'reng'/'ding' (plural subject articles). Attested in grammar descriptions of Kapampangan.

3rd-person plural genitive/non-subject enclitic pronoun — 'their' or 'by them'. Corresponds to Tagalog 'nila'. Not to be confused with the subject pronoun 'la' (they). Attested in existing pack examples: 'Sero ing bilang da' (numbers pack).

examples.kp: Metung a anak ing meyari ku.

entries.notes: The linker 'a' follows a word ending in a consonant. After a vowel, use '-ng' (suffix): 'bayu' → 'bayung tau' (new person). Equivalent to Tagalog 'na' or the '-ng' linker.

Pre-vocalic allomorph of 'ku' — used in the same positions (after predicate as subject enclitic, or possessive) but by many speakers interchangeably with 'ku'. 'Ko' is especially common before a pause or sentence-finally. Both 'ku' and 'ko' are correct Kapampangan; 'ko' is also the standard Tagalog 1sg genitive, which reinforces its frequency in modern speech.

1st person plural exclusive genitive/absolutive enclitic — 'our (not yours)'. The possessive and topic form of 'kami'. Used after a noun: 'bale mi' (our house), 'anak mi' (our child). Contrast with 'tamu/ta' (we inclusive, including the listener).

examples.en: The sea is far from here.
The school is far from here.

entries.notes: Proximal locative adverb — 'here' with a sense of 'from this location' or 'in relation to here'. Used as a sentence-final locative complement. Parallel to 'keti' (here, bare-locative) but 'kareni' often appears with distance words like 'malayo' (far) and 'malapit' (near). Equivalent to Tagalog 'dito / mula dito'.

Distal locative adverb — 'there' (away from both speaker and listener). Functions as a sentence-final locative predicate complement. Part of the Kapampangan demonstrative locative set: 'keti' (here, proximal), 'keta' (there, distal), 'kareni' (here, relational). Equivalent to Tagalog 'doon'.

Proximal locative adverb — 'here' (at this place). Used as a sentence-final locative complement. Part of the Kapampangan demonstrative locative set alongside 'keta' (there) and 'kareni' (here/from here). Equivalent to Tagalog 'dito'. Compare 'keni' (here — the oblique/dative locative used after 'king': 'king keni' = right here).

examples.kp: Maragul at malagu ya ing bale.
examples.en: The house is big and beautiful.

examples.kp: Maragul at mabangis ya ing damulag.
examples.en: The carabao is big and fierce.

entries.notes: Coordinating conjunction meaning 'and'. Shared with Tagalog. Used to connect adjectives, nouns, or clauses: 'Maragul at malagu' (big and beautiful). The native alternative 'ampong' exists but 'at' is standard in modern Kapampangan.

examples.kp: Munta ya i Elena king eskwela.
Magaral ya i Jose king iskuwela.

entries.notes: The Kapampangan personal article — used before proper names of people, equivalent to Tagalog 'si'. It marks the name as the topic of the sentence. 'Ing' is used before common nouns (thing/place); 'i' is used before personal names: 'i Elena', 'i Juan', 'i tatang ku' (my father).

examples.kp: Nung ati kang sakit, munta ka king doktor.
examples.en: If you are sick, go to the doctor.

entries.notes: Short/bare form of 'atin' — the existential predicate meaning 'there is' or 'to be at/present'. 'Atin' and 'ati' are used interchangeably by many speakers; 'ati' is more common as the bare predicate before a topic enclitic ('ati ka' = you are present), while 'atin' often precedes the noun phrase ('Atin ku kuwarta' = I have money). Contrast with 'atyu' (to be located, non-existential).

Alternant/variant of 'reng' — the absolutive plural article. 'Reng' and 'ring' are allomorphs used interchangeably by many speakers; 'ring' is common before back-vowel or nasal-initial words. Both function identically as the plural topic/absolutive article, equivalent to Tagalog 'ang mga'.

2nd person plural actor enclitic — marks 'you all' as the topic/agent of the sentence. The short actor form of 'kayu'. Used post-verbally: 'Saupan yu ku' (You all help me). Contrast with 'kayu' (the full independent form, used in predicate position).

2sg non-actor/agent enclitic in object-focus or goal-focus constructions — marks 'you' as the actor when the sentence is not actor-focused. Follows the verb directly. Distinct from 'mu' (2sg genitive possessive: 'bale mu' = your house) and 'me' (2sg actor focus: 'Sara me' = you close it). All three — mo/mu/me — are 2sg forms for different grammatical roles.

Distal demonstrative pronoun — 'that (one near the listener)'. Used after the linker 'a' to identify a person or thing: 'ing tau a yan' (that person). Part of the demonstrative series: iti/ini (this), yan (that, near listener), ita (that, away from both). Shared form with Tagalog 'yan/iyan' but is a native Kapampangan demonstrative.

Interrogative pronoun meaning 'what'. Functions as the predicate of a question: 'Ano ing X?' (What is the X?). The Kapampangan native equivalent is 'nanu', which is more formal/traditional; 'ano' is used interchangeably in modern speech and is shared with Tagalog. Both 'ano' and 'nanu' are attested in Kapampangan.

examples.en: Let's go to church on Sunday.

entries.notes: First-person inclusive plural actor enclitic — 'we (you and I together)'. Includes both the speaker and the listener. Equivalent to Tagalog 'tayo'. Distinct from 'kami' (exclusive 'we' — speaker + others, not the listener). 'Taku' is the actor/topic form; it often follows the verb directly as a post-verbal enclitic: 'Munta taku' (Let's go). Also analysable as 'ta' (1pl incl) + 'ku' (1sg).

examples.kp: Antayan tamu ya karing aduang oras.

entries.notes: Oblique plural article — marks a plural noun phrase as the oblique argument (direction, location, recipient, beneficiary). Formed from 'ka-' + 'ring' (plural absolutive). Contrast: 'king' (oblique singular, location/direction), 'ning' (genitive singular), 'karing' (oblique plural). Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa mga'.

Pre-vocalic allomorph of 'ku' (first-person singular actor/topic enclitic). Used when the following word begins with a vowel, to avoid the awkward sequence 'ku + vowel'. Example: 'Bisa ku' (I want) vs 'Bisa kung mangan' (I want to eat — 'mangan' starts with /m/, but the -ng forms when a vowel-initial complement follows). Also marks possession before vowel-initial nouns. Learners should treat 'ku' and 'kung' as the same word in different phonological contexts.

An alternative 2nd-person plural absolutive pronoun alongside 'kayu'. Derived from Proto-Malayo-Polynesian *kamu(ʔ) (you plural), making it the etymologically older form. In contemporary Kapampangan, 'kayu' is more standard and also carries the polite singular function; 'kamu' appears in informal speech and in older or more traditional registers. Learners should prioritize 'kayu' but recognize 'kamu' when encountered.

examples.en: The two of us will go to the market.
The two of us have work today.

entries.notes: First-person dual inclusive pronoun — speaker plus exactly one other person. Equivalent to Tagalog 'tayong dalawa'. Distinct from 'tamu' (we inclusive, more than two) and 'kami' (we exclusive). Relatively rare in modern speech but important for completeness.
Oblique (prepositional) first-person singular pronoun. Used after prepositions like 'para' (for) and directional verbs. Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa akin'. Contrast with 'ku' (absolutive/genitive I/my).

Emphatic first-person singular pronoun. Used when the speaker wants to stress personal agency or contrast: 'I (and not someone else)'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'ako mismo'. Often appears with 'ku' in the same clause: 'Sake ku...' = 'I myself...'.

Distal/remote demonstrative pronoun meaning 'those (far from both speaker and listener)'. Part of the Kapampangan tri-level demonstrative system: 'iti' (this, near speaker), 'iyan'/'yan' (that, near listener), 'dita' (those, remote from both). 'Dita' is the plural/general remote form. Compare 'ita' (that one, singular remote). Equivalent to Tagalog 'yoon' (remote) or 'iyon'.

examples.kp: Atyu la ri Pedro king bale.
examples.en: Pedro and the others are at the house.

entries.notes: Plural personal article marking a named person together with their associates ('X and company'). The d~/r~ variant 'di' is interchangeable; 'ring' and 'ding' are the same marker with the '-ng' linker attached before a following word.

lessons.title: Absolutive Pronouns
lessons.theme: Personal pronouns (ang-set): ka, kayu, la, kami
lessons.intro: In Kapampangan, personal pronouns come in two major sets. The absolutive set (sometimes called the 'ang-set') marks the topic or subject of the sentence. These pronouns are enclitics — they attach after the predicate, not before it. In this lesson you will learn: ka (you, singular), kayu (you, plural or polite), la (they), and kami (we, not including you). You already know ku (I), ya (he/she), and tamu (we inclusive).

lessons.title: Singular Articles: ing & ning
lessons.intro: Kapampangan uses articles (like English 'the') that change depending on whether the noun is the topic (subject) of the sentence or not. For singular nouns: 'ing' marks the topic noun — the word the sentence is 'about' — much like Tagalog 'ang'. The word 'ning' marks non-topic singular nouns (possessors, objects), like Tagalog 'ng'. Both can translate to English 'the' or 'a' depending on context.

accepted: the eyes of the woman are beautiful.
the eyes of the woman are beautiful

exercises.prompt: Maragul ing bale ____ mayaman. (of the — non-topic)

lessons.title: Plural Articles: reng, ding & deng
lessons.intro: For plural nouns, Kapampangan uses 'reng' or 'ding' to mark the topic noun (Tagalog: ang mga), and 'deng' to mark a non-topic noun (Tagalog: ng mga). The articles 'reng' and 'ding' are the same word in two phonological forms: 'ding' is the base form used after vowel-final words, 'reng' appears after consonant-final words. In everyday speech, both are used fairly freely. The pronoun 'da' (their / by them) is the plural genitive pronoun that pairs naturally with these articles.

exercises.prompt: Which article marks a plural non-topic noun (like Tagalog "ng mga")?
Mura ding gulay king palengke ngeni.
accepted: vegetables are cheap at the market today.
vegetables are cheap at the market today
the vegetables are cheap at the market today.
the vegetables are cheap at the market today

lessons.title: The Linker: a / -ng
lessons.intro: Kapampangan uses the linker 'a' to connect an adjective or number to the noun it describes: 'mayap a tau' (good person), 'atlung aldo' (three days). After a vowel-final word, the linker is '-ng' (a suffix), not a separate word.

exercises.prompt: What is the function of "a" in "mayap a tau"?
Which particle links an adjective to a noun after a consonant?

lessons.title: More Pronouns, Articles, and Conjunctions
lessons.intro: 'ko' is the allomorph of 'ku' used interchangeably by many speakers. 'mi' means our (exclusive — not including the listener). 'at' connects words and clauses with 'and'. 'i' is the personal article placed before a person's name (like Tagalog 'si'). 'ati' is the bare existential predicate: 'Ati ka keni?' (Are you here?).

exercises.prompt: Munta ya i Elena king eskwela.
Maragul ____ malagu ya ing bale. (The house is big and beautiful.)
Obakit ____ ka keni? (Why are you here?)

lessons.title: Locative Adverbs: kareni, keta, keti
lessons.intro: 'keti' and 'kareni' both mean 'here', while 'keta' means 'there'. These sentence-final locatives place people and things in space without needing 'king'. 'Malapit ya ing tindahan keti' — the store is near here. 'Maragul ya ing dutung keta' — the tree there is big.

accepted: the sea is far from here
the sea is far from here.
the ocean is far from here
the ocean is far from here.

exercises.prompt: Maragul ya ing dutung ____. (The tree there is big.)
Malayo ya ing iskuwela ____. (The school is far from here.)

lessons.title: More Articles, Pronouns, and Questions
lessons.intro: 'ring' is an alternant of 'reng' (plural article). 'yu' is the 2pl actor enclitic (you all do X). 'mo' marks 2sg agency in non-actor-focus constructions (Lawen mo = you look). 'yan' is the distal demonstrative 'that'. 'Ano' means 'what'.

exercises.prompt: Adwa ____ Kapatad ku. (I have two siblings.)
Lawen ____ reng tau keti. (Look at the people here.)
Saupan ____ ku pu. (Please help me, you all.)

lessons.title: Inclusive We, Oblique Plural, and Prevocalic I
lessons.intro: 'taku' is the first-person inclusive plural (we = you + I). 'karing' is the oblique plural article (to/at/for the plural). 'kung' is the prevocalic form of 'ku' (I/me), used when followed by a vowel-initial word.

accepted: let's go to church on sunday
let's go to church on sunday.
let us go to church on sunday
let us go to church on sunday.

exercises.prompt: Munta ____ simbahan king duminggu. (Let's go to church on Sunday.)
Ibili yu ____ anak. (Buy for the children.)

lessons.title: Informal 2nd-person Plural: kamu
lessons.intro: 'Kamu' is an informal 2nd-person plural pronoun ('you all') derived from Proto-Malayo-Polynesian *kamu. In modern Kapampangan, 'kayu' is more standard; 'kamu' appears in informal speech and traditional registers.

lessons.title: Dual & Oblique: kata, kanaku, sake
lessons.theme: dual inclusive pronoun, oblique 1sg, and emphatic 1sg
lessons.intro: Three specialized first-person pronouns: 'kata' (we two — dual inclusive, speaker + one other), 'kanaku' (to me/for me — oblique prepositional form), and 'sake' (I myself — emphatic). Together these fill out the Kapampangan pronoun system beyond the basic 'ku' and 'tamu'.

exercises.prompt: Which word means "to me" or "for me"?
Which word means "we two" or "the two of us"?

accepted: the two of us will go to the market
the two of us will go to the market.
we two will go to the market
we two will go to the market.

lessons.title: Remote Demonstrative: dita
lessons.intro: 'dita' is the Kapampangan remote demonstrative pronoun — it points to things far from both speaker and listener, equivalent to 'those (over there)'. The three-level system: 'iti' (this, near me), 'yan' (that, near you), 'dita' (those, far away).

lessons.title: People in the Plural: ri / di
lessons.intro: 'Ri' (and its twin 'di') marks a named person together with their companions — 'Juan and the others'. Add the '-ng' linker and you get the familiar 'ring' / 'ding'.

exercises.prompt: What does the marker "ri" (as in "ri Juan") signal?
Which marker means "X and company" before a name?
Munta la ____ Juan. (Juan and the others went.)
Atyu la ri Pedro king bale.
accepted: pedro and the others are at the house
pedro and the others are at the house.
pedro and company are at the house
pedro and company are at the house.
